caring.definition: Showing that you think about and want to help someone.
caring.example: A caring parent listens to their child's problems.

goodwill.definition: Friendly and kind feelings towards someone.
goodwill.example: Goodwill between partners helps the whole family.

respect.definition: Treating someone in a way that shows you value them.
respect.example: Respect between parents creates a safe home.

contribute.definition: To help cause something to happen.
contribute.example: Love and respect contribute to a child's sense of safety.

strain.definition: Pressure or stress that makes something difficult.
strain.example: Having a new baby puts a strain on your relationship.

spontaneity.definition: Doing things suddenly without planning, just because you feel like it.
spontaneity.example: Spontaneity in a relationship keeps it exciting.

compromised.definition: Made weaker or less good than before.
compromised.example: Their freedom was compromised after the baby arrived.

diminished.example: Time alone with friends was diminished after having children.

reduced.definition: Made less in amount or size.
reduced.example: Free time was reduced to almost nothing.

disappear.example: Your free time may disappear completely.

disrupted.definition: Broken up or changed from the normal pattern.
disrupted.example: Sleep patterns will be disrupted by the new baby.

significantly.definition: By a large amount; in an important way.
significantly.example: You will have to manage on significantly less sleep.

extended.definition: Including more people beyond the immediate family.
extended.example: The extended family includes aunts, uncles, and cousins.

philosophies.definition: Beliefs or ideas about how something should be done.
philosophies.example: Parents may have different parenting philosophies.

dynamics.definition: The way people in a group affect each other.
dynamics.example: The dynamics in the family changed after the baby came.

shift.definition: To change or move from one state to another.
shift.example: The dynamics within the family may shift.

alter.example: Stopping work may alter how you see yourself.

colleagues.example: She missed her former colleagues after leaving her job.

recede.definition: To move away or become less present.
recede.example: Some friends may seem to recede for a while.

preoccupation.definition: Being so focused on one thing that you forget about other things.
preoccupation.example: Her preoccupation with the baby left little time for friends.

exhaustive.example: This is by no means an exhaustive list.

transition.definition: A change from one state or situation to another.
transition.example: The transition to becoming a family is a big change.

resentment.definition: A feeling of anger because you think something is unfair.
resentment.example: Resentment between parents hurts the whole family.

admit.definition: To say that something is true, even if it is hard.
admit.example: It is better to admit your resentment than to hide it.

justify.definition: To give reasons for doing something, often when it was wrong.
justify.example: If you hide your feelings, you may justify bad behavior.

responsibility.definition: The duty to deal with something or take care of someone.
responsibility.example: Both parents share responsibility for their child.

static.example: Life is never static; things always change.

embrace.definition: To accept something willingly and happily.
embrace.example: Try to embrace the changes that come with a new baby.

flexible.definition: Able to change and adjust easily.
flexible.example: Being flexible helps you deal with surprises.

effective.definition: Working well and getting the result you want.
effective.example: Being flexible is more effective than fighting change.

regain.definition: To get back something you lost.
regain.example: Trying to regain your old life may not work.

surrendering.definition: Giving up fighting against something and accepting it.
surrendering.example: Surrendering to your new life can bring peace.

resented.definition: Felt angry about something because it seemed unfair.
resented.example: He resented how much his life had changed.

upbringing.definition: The way you were raised and cared for as a child.
upbringing.example: His upbringing shaped how he felt about being a father.

tracing.definition: Following something back to find where it started.
tracing.example: He found the source of his anger by tracing it to his childhood.

childcare.definition: The care and looking after of children.
childcare.example: He found meaning in childcare instead of seeing it as boring.

chore.definition: A task that you have to do, often boring or unpleasant.
chore.example: He stopped seeing childcare as a boring chore.

joint.definition: Shared by two or more people together.
joint.example: They accepted joint responsibility for their child.

equal.definition: The same in amount, level, or importance.
equal.example: Both parents should have equal responsibility.

freed.definition: Released or allowed to be themselves.
freed.example: Sharing the work freed her up to be more of her old self.

wholly.example: She was wholly preoccupied with the baby.
